author: @aphilipdent

author: @FredRose2001
text: Yes a chimney is a complex pice of engineering, one of the old American presidents from the 1800s wrote a book on how to build brick a chimney that works, that draws and no back draft, in my country there was a house that was demolished all but the fireplace and chimney, it was a work of art, it was built out of house bricks laid in a circular pattern ( to make a smooth tube on the inner surface ) but with a twist all the way up, I doubt if there a bricklayer that has the skills and knowledge to do it today.

author: @JamesBlower
text:
Can't see it working, but you never know, my gut feeling is to trap the solar energy (that can be upto 1kw per square metre, in a twin walled container with a rotor & vacuum in the middle and maybe spin a magnetly levitated & coupled rotor to the outside, so no seal to create friction to slow the rotor, 
The rotor could be black on the back of the blades & reflective on the front to create the turning force from the heated molecules leaving the black surface, the vacuum will reduce the drag on the rotor.
But then again no moving parts is probably more logical, a solar kettle maybe, I don't think 3d printers are upto the job yet, mind you we now have Ai to design them with so you never know. Good luck with project.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't think you should see it as a replacement for solar cells - but as solar cells overheat - it might be an idea to use it with them in order to provide passive cooling of the cells alongside energy scavenging from the heat that would be wasted

replies.author: @JamesBlower
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I have noticed that my solar voltaic solar panels work better on cold days, maybe a passive solar powered cooling system that moves air could be made to pull cool air across the panels as the hot air leaves a chimney.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text: My design is far superior.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - did you share it?

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ Nah... 1, I was actually seeking financing to produce it so I am under contract, and 2, its a very complicated design that took years to perfect.

It is not merely a shape, its a full system.

As much as I respect you sharing what you are doing, not everybody will share the fine details they work a long time on.

When a person passes on [as likely I will some day] maybe somebody will find my designs and share them, if they know what they are looking at, and feel driven to share it, I won't be around to complain, so it may get out.

But yeah, some of what I have spent years researching, I am not really driven to pass this along, especially since many will [as happens with all major inventions] mock it and feel compelled to give me grief over it.

I figure its best to keep it to myself, at least until I make a working demonstrator model of it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542 that's fair enough mate

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
I was thinking about something like this awhile back. One thing you'd want to do is, for the areas you want to get hot, paint them a mat black. I was thinking about something like black3.
The areas you want to keep cool should be painted white, which is basically a scattered reflection. You can then save your mirror reflectors for the areas where you are trying to aim the light and heat to the black areas.

author: @TheDrrbc
text:
Been looking at this for a while. Best idea so far is to repurpose used corrugated tin painted black, formed into a tube, rising 60 feet over about 150-200 yards, and terminating in something like your DIY rooftop generator. Figure maybe 5% efficiency for 5-6 hours in the daytime. So maybe 35kW/d?

What would be interesting is if the process could be reversed by nighttime radiative cooling.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i was pondering that roof generator too!

author: @GabrielPettier
text: Really cool idea, not sure how hot the air need to be for the effect to move a turbine, maybe concentrating the heat would risk damaging the 3D printed structure, i guess in the theory most of the structure could be made large scale with cast concrete or smaller scale with wood or metal sheets indeed, as most of the parts don't move. Maybe this could be coupled with a mirror system similar to concentrated solar power. As the real important thing here is the temperature difference between inside and outside of the engine, maybe it could be a more reliable source of power than other renewables, and quite low-tech, too.

author: @POKERDEAL
text: I have built loads of chimneys on houses and they have a draw on them even without a fire in them . Normally they have a bend in them to improve draw . We test the draw with smoke pellets . Maybe you could build a mini wind wall in your own living room . Obviously no two chimneys are the same tho

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: good idea mate - give it a try

replies.author: @Debbie-henri
replies.text:
@POKERDEAL Oh, I wondered why there was a bend in the chimney not far above our wood stove. It was a devil of a job trying to get the chimney liner up through it.
Our wood stove has an openable/closeable slotted plate above the door to the fire. I suppose this does the same thing as the slots in the solar heater here. It certainly works better than waiting for the sun to come out in our sitting room, at night.

 I wonder how this solar heater could be used? Heat a greenhouse, conservatory or glass roofed summerhouse? It would have to be vented into a house, I'd presume. 
Think I'll stick to firewood for indoors. It washes downstream for free most of the time.

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
curious about the 'per square meter' efficiency compared to a solar panel.  
down the line is the 'cost per square meter' which can be expensive for solar panels and cheap for plastic/metal tube..  even with the increased cost of a focusing lens of some sort, that can be done pretty cheap and might benefit the heated air design more than a solar panel which tends to have a limit of how much it can take in before getting hot and losing efficiency..
the hot air turbine would seem to just increase in efficiency.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't thin you should see it as a replacement for solar cells - but as solar cells overheat - it might be an idea to use it with them in order to provide passive cooling of the cells alongside energy scavenging from the heat that would be wasted

author: @jamesross1003
text: That solar vortex turbine is something that I shared a design by description in a comment on your channel years ago Rob.  Most important part that I found was the fact that when you used a dark brick or some other dark heat store as the floor of your "green house part of the design" and the correct chimney size for your heat output as well as a top turbine that also caught the wind would significantly increase it's total end torque.  A squirrel cage type turbine that also had internal blades as well as the cage(internally as inside the squirrel cage and not inside the chimney worked well.  The warmed air would catch on the internal blades and the cage would catch the wind.  Both complimented each other giving additive torque and it would also turn with either just wind or rising air so it would work rather or not the wind was blowing and rather or not the sun was out.  Residual heat in the brick/heat store would extend the operation time and after it reached it's cooling stage past equilibrium it would turn in reverse also giving voltage in that direction.  Voltage regulators and rectifiers are a must, but with experimentation this works quite well.  The extended run time with or without wind makes up for the dead time a traditional wind turbine or solar panel has.  Sure the traditional type does put out more in bursts but this type design makes up for it over time.  Cheers!  Thanks for the video!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - thank you for sharing mate and for the tips

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks for the response!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering You can also use a Peltier device under the floor and store the power from it to give a bump to your turbine at it's start up time during following cycles to counter any pauses during the complete equilibrium phase of operation.  It will come to a stop for a short time before it reverses.   Use that stored energy to give a small push to the turbine.  Saves on even more down time. <------just a side note for my future designs improvements.  Cheers!

Now it's just a 3 month game of merry go round to actually get them to fix their mistakes...

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
I suspect one of the main reasons is that if you suck out too much heat your chimney no longer works! 
There is a commercial product available that blows air from your room through a small bore pipe system installed in your riser (the bit of the chimney before the flue on a wood burner) As with all things it is about the balance being right but the heat output into the room was substantial!
